hub_subtitle: Business English para as reuniões com o time Global da Sanofi, congressos médicos e a apresentação de agosto

lesson.menu_title: What Comes Next? -- Forecasting Brazil's Results for the Global Team
lesson.menu_desc: Depois de reportar o passado, o passo que a Global sempre pede a seguir: a PREVISÃO. A aula do 'will' -- a linguagem do forecast -- para dizer o que você espera, prevê e projeta dos números do Brasil, com honestidade até no shortfall -- 37 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 16 -- What Comes Next?
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Emmanuele Orrico | Lesson 16 -- What Comes Next?

listenings.text: Hi Emmanuele, David here from the Global team. Quick ask before Thursday's review. This time we don't only want the Q3 results. We want your forecast for Q4. Two numbers, that's all. First, what increase do you predict for the private market? And second, where do you see the public market, on target or a small shortfall? Keep it simple, just your best estimate. Nobody expects a perfect number. We only want to know where you think we will be at the end of the year. Thanks. Talk on Thursday.

Okay, let me wrap up the forecast part. Here is where we landed. For the private market, Emmanuele predicts a steady increase, around eight percent, into next quarter. For the public market, we expect a small shortfall this quarter, but the projection for the first quarter is positive, so it will recover. So, the action points. Emmanuele will send the updated forecast slide by Friday, and we will review the full-year estimate next month. Good work, everyone. The numbers are honest, and honest numbers are the ones we can plan with.

extra_audio.text: My projection for Q4 is positive. I predict sales will increase by about eight percent. After the peak in September, the numbers will stay steady. The public market will have a small shortfall this quarter. But it will recover in the first quarter, so the full-year estimate is strong.

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: a number you expect in the future, based on your data
an approximate number, not the exact one
to say what you think will happen
slow and regular, without big changes
when you have less than the plan

reading.rtitle: Reading the Future in the Numbers
paras: Every quarter, Emmanuele presents the Brazil results to the Global team. For a long time, she only talked about the past: what happened, what the numbers were. That is important. But the Global team always wants one more thing -- the future. They ask: "And next quarter? What do you predict?" A good manager does not only report results. She gives a projection.

A projection is not a promise. It is your best estimate of what will happen, based on the data you have. "Sales will increase in the second half." "The public market will grow slowly, but it will grow." "I think this year will be our best year." Notice the small word in every sentence: will. Will is the language of the forecast. You use it to say what you expect, what you predict, what you think will happen -- not what is already decided (that is "going to"), but what the numbers point to.

Of course, a good forecast is honest. Some months are a peak; then the numbers decrease a little and stay steady. And sometimes you predict an increase, but you also have to report a shortfall -- a number below the plan. "We will probably miss the target by a small amount this quarter." That is not weakness; that is control. The manager who can say "here is where we are, and here is where I think we will be" is the manager the Global team trusts. The past is the story. The forecast is the value.

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: A good manager should only report the past results and never guess about the future.
A good manager gives a forecast -- what she predicts will happen -- not only past results, and "will" is the language of that forecast.
A projection is a promise, so you must always reach exactly the number you say.

items: The text says a good manager only reports the past results.
f
The opposite: "A good manager does not only report results. She gives a projection."
According to the text, a projection is a promise.
f
The text says: "A projection is not a promise. It is your best estimate of what will happen."
The text says you use "will" to say what you expect or predict in the future.
t
"Will is the language of the forecast. You use it to say what you expect, what you predict, what you think will happen."
According to the text, reporting a shortfall is a sign of weakness.
f
The text says: "That is not weakness; that is control."

parts: increase by about eight percent, and stay
after the peak. For the public market, we will have a small
, but it will recover in Q1."

gapfill.text: The whole forecast runs on one small word: WILL. "Sales will increase." "It will stay steady." "It will recover." After will, you always use the base verb -- no "to", no "-s": will increase, will grow, will be (never "will to increase", never "will grows"). Keep going to for the plan you already decided ("We are going to launch in Q3"), and use will for what you PREDICT from the numbers ("I think it will be a success").

bank.label: Useful language for a forecast
items: My projection for next quarter is positive.
I predict a steady increase of about eight percent.
I think this year will be stronger than last year.
We will probably have a small shortfall, but it will recover.
This is only an estimate, not the final number.
After the peak, the numbers will stay steady.
Where do you think we will be at the end of the year?

lf.title: Which is it -- a prediction, a decided plan, or an estimate?
items: A
Sales will increase next quarter.
-- a prediction
strong
B
We are going to launch in Q3.
-- a decided plan
soft
C
I think it will stay steady.
-- a prediction
strong
D
This is about five percent, more or less.
-- an estimate
soft

lf.text: A and C use WILL -- they say what you PREDICT, what you expect from the numbers. B uses GOING TO -- a plan already decided, not a guess. D is an estimate -- an approximate number, not an exact one. A real forecast mixes all three: the plan you decided, the numbers you predict with will, and the honest "more or less".

items.situation: The Global team asks about next quarter's sales. Give your prediction.
tips: I think sales will increase by about eight percent.
My projection for next quarter is a steady increase.

items.situation: They ask if the growth will be steady. Answer.
tips: Yes, I predict it will stay steady after the peak in September.
It will be steady, not up and down.

items.situation: The public market is below plan. Report the shortfall honestly, and add hope.
tips: We will probably have a small shortfall this quarter, but it will recover in Q1.
I predict a shortfall of about five percent, and then it will improve.

items.situation: They ask for the exact number, but you only have an approximate one.
tips: This is only an estimate, but I predict around five percent growth.
I don't have the final number yet -- my best estimate is five percent.

items.situation: Compare this year to last year in one prediction.
tips: I think this year will be stronger than last year.
My projection is that this year will beat last year.

items.situation: Close your forecast with one confident line.
tips: Overall, the projection is positive -- I believe we will reach the full-year target.
The numbers point up, so I think we will finish the year strong.
